coredata.dc:title: Exploring the ability of water soluble carbon dots as matrix for detecting neurological disorders using MALDI-TOF MS
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Shahnawaz Khan, M.
Bhaisare, Mukesh L.
Pandey, Sunil
Talib, Abou
Wu, Shou-Mei
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
Wu, Hui-Fen
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (C-dots) exhibit strong absorbance in the UV (220–350nm) range, which was exploited to transfer the energy from N2 laser (337nm) of matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization-mass spectrometry (MALDI-MS) to analytes for their rapid detection. Due to this strong feature and extremely small size (2–4nm), they were used to enhance the signal intensity of MALDI-MS peaks of low molecular weight biomarkers in serum. In this study, we utilized the extraordinary property of C-dots as a matrix for the detection of serotonin (Sr), glutamic acid (GA) and dopamine hydrochloride (DA) by using MALDI-MS. These chemicals are cardinal biological indicators or biomarkers for the detection of dreadful disorders like Alzheimer's disease. The limit of detection of Sr, GA and DA was found to be 3, 5 and 8nM, respectively. The background noises in MALDI-MS spectra can be significantly reduced by using C-dots which are superior to the conventional matrices such as 2,5-dihydroxy benzoic acid (DHB). The current approach can be further applied to many other drugs or pharmaceutical compounds in the near future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Matrix-assisted laser desorption-ionization time-of-flight mass spectroscopy
Serotonin
Glutamic acid
Dopamine hydrochloride
2,5-Dihydroxybenzoic acid (DHB)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantification of Sr, GA and DA in serum
Study of C-dots as a matrix by MALDI-MS

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Sun Yat-Sen University and Kaohsiung Medical University Joint Research Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the National Sun Yat-Sen University and Kaohsiung Medical University Joint Research Project (NSYSU-KMU JOINT RESEARCH PROJECT), #NSYSU-KMU (104-I 006) for financial support for this publication. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis spectra of C-dots also showing photographs under normal light and UV light. (b) Showing the fluorescence of C-dots. (c) TEM image of purified C-dots.
(a) Raman, (b) XRD spectra of purified C-dots displaying their typical morphological features.
FTIR spectra of C-dots showing their typical functionalization pattern.

(A) MALDI-MS spectra of dopamine hydrochloride with (a) bare C-dots, (b) DHB matrix [♦], (c) DHB-dopamine hydrochloride and (d) C-dots in dopamine hydrochloride [★]. (B) MALDI-MS spectra of dopamine hydrochloride with (a) bare C-dots, (b) DHB matrix [♦], (c) DHB-dopamine hydrochloride in serum and (d) C-dots-dopamine hydrochloride in serum [★].

(A) Detection of serotonin by using DHB and C-dots. MALDI MS spectra of (a) C-dots, (b) DHB matrix [♦], (c) DHB-serotonin and (d) C-dots-serotonin [★]. (B) Detection of serotonin by using DHB and C-dots. MALDI MS spectra of (a) C-dots, (b) DHB matrix [♦], (c) DHB-serotonin in serum and (d) C-dots-serotonin in serum [★].

(A) MALDI-MS spectra of glutamic acid using (a) pure C-dots, (b) DHB matrix [♦], (c) DHB-glutamic acid and (d) C-dots-glutamic acid [★]. (B) MALDI-MS spectra of glutamic acid in human serum using (a) pure C-dots, (b) DHB matrix [♦], (c) DHB-glutamic acid and (d) C-dots-glutamic acid in serum [★].
Tabulated all peaks in MALDI-MS spectrum of serotonin, glutamic acid and dopamine hydrochloride in C-dots matrix vs DHB matrix.
Determination of LOD (nM) for serotonin, glutamic acid and dopamine hydrochloride by using two matrices.

head.ce:title: Exploring the ability of water soluble carbon dots as matrix for detecting neurological disorders using MALDI-TOF MS

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (C-dots) exhibit strong absorbance in the UV (220–350nm) range, which was exploited to transfer the energy from N2 laser (337nm) of matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization-mass spectrometry (MALDI-MS) to analytes for their rapid detection. Due to this strong feature and extremely small size (2–4nm), they were used to enhance the signal intensity of MALDI-MS peaks of low molecular weight biomarkers in serum. In this study, we utilized the extraordinary property of C-dots as a matrix for the detection of serotonin (Sr), glutamic acid (GA) and dopamine hydrochloride (DA) by using MALDI-MS. These chemicals are cardinal biological indicators or biomarkers for the detection of dreadful disorders like Alzheimer's disease. The limit of detection of Sr, GA and DA was found to be 3, 5 and 8nM, respectively. The background noises in MALDI-MS spectra can be significantly reduced by using C-dots which are superior to the conventional matrices such as 2,5-dihydroxy benzoic acid (DHB). The current approach can be further applied to many other drugs or pharmaceutical compounds in the near future.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Matrix-assisted laser desorption-ionization time-of-flight mass spectroscopy

ce:section.ce:para: The sensitive detection and quantification of biomarkers for the life threatening diseases such as neurological disorders or cancers can make a significant contribution to theranostics. One of the pivotal contributions of nanotechnology towards this goal is to develop modern nanomaterials capable of sensing low concentration of biomarkers in body fluids, which contain plethora of interfering proteins and ions. Advances in mass spectrometry during last several decades have completely revolutionized the fields of diagnosis and treatment related with incurable neurological ailments like Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, dementia, etc. One of the most essential parameters for sensing analytes in complex biological fluids requires novel matrices which can help for better confrontation between laser as well as analytes detected by MALDI-MS [1]. It is an important tool in deciphering the rapid analysis of biomolecules. It has been used extensively in deciphering the codes of large molecules. Despite the remarkable performance of MALDI-MS, it has some limitations in analyzing low molecular weight compounds due to many background interferences by using traditional organic matrices. During the analysis, matrices play an imperative role in absorbing and transferring the energy from N2 (337nm) laser to analytes for effective ionization [2]. Conventional matrices such as 2,5-dihydroxy benzoic acids (2,5-DHB), sinapinic acid (SA), α-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid (CHCA), picolinic acid (PA), and 3-hydroxy picolinic acid (HPA) suffer from production of fragments and clusters and sweet spots. Moreover, non-homogenous mixing of matrices with analytes often generate interferes during the analysis.

Recently, a close cousin of graphene called carbon dots (C-dots) as a matrix for MALDI-MS have been glorified to a great extent due to physical properties of self-passivation [3]. The high optical absorbance of C-dots at ∼337nm makes them a favorite choice to be used as a matrix for detecting the low molecular weight biomarkers in MALDI-MS [3]. The energy absorbed by C-dots coincides with the N2 laser used in MALDI-MS to ionize and desorbs analytes, so sufficient laser energy can be transferred to analytes to make the C-dots as a superior matrix over the conventional matrices. In addition, C-dots have been also used intensively in drug delivery, biosensors and optical properties of biological applications [4] owing to their excellent physical–chemical properties like self-passivation, high surface area to volume ratios, good solubility in water and organic solvent [5]. In this study, we exploited the excellent properties of C-dots as a matrix for the detection and quantification of several important drugs/biomarkers [6]. Other inorganic MALDI-MS matrices such as graphitized carbon black, carbon nanotubes (CNT) [7], gold [8], graphene paper [9], fullerenes, graphene sheets [10,11] and graphite plate (laser) [12,13] are of lower anisotropic nature and stabile under high vacuum compared with the C-dots. It is also exploited for the determination of critical micelle concentration of ionic and nonionic surfactants [14]. Previously, C-dots were applied as a matrix for detecting mefenamic acid and compared with DHB matrix which is mostly used for low molecular weight compound in MALDI-MS [15]. Herein, we introduce the water-soluble C-dots for the detection of essential biomarkers (Sr, GA and DA) which are directly related with Alzheimer diseases. Sensitive and precise detection of such biomarkers can play a significant role in prognosis of the disease as well as their treatment.

DA is the most important neurotransmitter in central nervous system (CNS). It has also significant role in Parkinson's disease and drug addiction. Many researchers have tried to find the concentration of DA by using [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and reached to a concentration of 7.6×10−7 M [16]. GA is an important neurotransmitter as well as indicator of the Alzheimer disease. It plays an important role in brain formation, neuronal differentiation, migration and survival [17]. Stimulation of glutamate-gated ion channels causes neurological degeneration which can cause necrosis or apoptosis [18]. This may result in Alzheimer diseases, Huntington's diseases, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) stroke. It has been used to treat mental retardation, epilepsy, Parkinson's disease, and muscular dystrophy. Another important biomarker which is selected in our study is Sr which is a monoamine neurotransmitter popularly regarded as a feeling of peace and happiness [19]. Lack of this chemical in the human body may result in grievous psychiatric symptoms. Abnormality in serotonergic may affect the function of dorso-lateral prefrontal cortex which in the apex of cognition and emotion in the Alzheimer diseases. Because of the above important reasons, it is necessary to detect and analyze these biomarkers. Early detection is an important step in rapid diagnosis of many dreadful diseases. Proteins and other molecules can be a source of clue to find out its presence by using a promising analytical instrument called MALDI-MS. It was developed by Karas in 1988 for analysis and detection of biological samples, polymer, biomarkers and drugs in tissue analysis of fatty acids [20–22]. In this work, it is used for the detection of analyte as it enhances the soft ionization of analytes and gives significant biomarker peaks. Based on the proposed studies, the C-dots proved excellent matrix with respect to commonly used DHB matrix. C-dots as a matrix show the less background signals with high intensity of peaks. Biomarkers were quantified and spiked in different concentrations in serum by using the standard calibration curves. We also tried to explore the use of C-dots for determining the high molecular weight compounds but it shows lot of cumbersome peaks in this range, which were not reproducible. In the present work, C-dots were used as an excellent matrix for the MALDI-MS analysis in exploring the low molecular weight compounds. By using C-dots as a matrix, MALDI-MS spectra show less background or noises, sharp peaks and shot to shot reproducibility which are helpful in exploring the information of biological materials. Carboxylic acids as a functional group present on the surface of C-dots help in high water solubility, which helps in fast dispersion in making homogenous solution. Thereby, help in making excellent co-crystallization, due to small size it can easily cover the analyte which helps in getting good signals as well as sensitivity. Moreover, C-dots are easy to synthesize, have good stability, are biocompatible and cheap. These properties show that they are good candidates as an excellent matrix in analyzing low molecular weight compound.

Acetonitrile (ACN), anhydrous citric acid and chloroform were purchased from J.T.Baker, USA. Trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) was purchased from Wako Pure Chemicals (Osaka, Japan). Serotonin (Sr), glutamic acid (GA) and dopamine hydrochloride (DA), and 2,5-dihydroxy benzoic acid (DHB) were purchased from Sigma. The water used for the experimental purpose including the cleaning of glass wares was used from a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA).

In ultra-pure water, 10g of citric acid was dissolved and stirred to make a homogeneous solution. This solution was refluxed for 12h at 60°C till the solution turned yellowish red. Subsequently C-dots were purified by 20mL of the solution subjected to micron-filtration using 0.45μm nylon filter using Millipore filtration kit (Millipore, USA). Bright yellowish green solution was obtained after the filtration. Finally the powder of C-dots after vacuum drying of its solution at 120°C was obtained. 15mg of cysteamine hydrochloride was dissolved in 5mL of DI water and 40μL was measured from this stock solution and added in 1mL of pure C-dots and total volume was adjusted to 3mL, and was kept for 3h under stirring. After preliminary confirmation under UV light (λ =365nm) to check the blue color, the solution was scrutinized by UV-Vis Spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer Lambda 25, USA) to comprehend its optical properties.

Microflex MALDI-MS (Bruker Daltonics, Germany) was used for the analysis of analyte biomarker's peaks and serum peaks. All the data were recorded triplicate in linear/positive-ion mode. MS system was operated under accelerating voltage of 20kV and using a pulse nitrogen laser (337nm, 4-ns pulses at 10.0Hz, 60.0μJ). 96-wells MALDI-MS target plate was used and MALDI-MS spectra were taken by applying 150 laser shots. The aqueous solutions of as prepared C-dots were mixed with DA, GA and Sr in equal amounts by volume (1μL of C-dots with 1μL of analytes). Same number of laser shots was also in consideration during the analysis on MALDI-MS plate.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, and Japan) was applied for studies of shape and size of C-dots. Carbon coated grids were drop casted using dilute solution of C-dots for the sample analysis. Surface passivation of C-dots was studied using Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) after drop coating C-dots solution on KBr pellets. The UV–vis. spectra of C-dots were recorded by double beam UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA) over 200–800nm wavelength ranges. Raman spectroscopy Agiltron Peak seeker of US was used. Fluorescence spectra of C-dots were acquired from fluorescence spectroscopy (Hitachi, F-2700, Japan) at various (λ ext =200, 250, 300, 350nm) excitation values.

A stock standard solution of Sr (1mM), GA (1mM) and DA (1mM) was made by dissolving 1.76mg, 1.89mg and 1.46mg in 10mL of double distilled water respectively. The solution of C-dots was prepared in double distilled water at the concentration of 0.1mgmL−1. Dihydroxy benzoic acid (DHB) matrix (50mM) was prepared in acetonitrile/deionized water (2:1, v/v) and adding 0.1% trifluoric acid (TFA). For MALDI-MS analysis 2.0μL of the analyte was vortexed with equal volume of 2.0μL of C-dot's solution. From this mixture, 1μL was spotted on the MALDI-MS stainless plate and dried at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of Sr, GA and DA in serum
ce:section.ce:para: Adult male albino mice with a mean weight 20±5g were obtained from the Bio Lasco Co. Ltd, Taipei, Taiwan and kept at room temperature (25°C) with a 12h light/dark cycle. Mice were provided with food and water. The ethics committee of National Sun Yat-Sen University, Taiwan has approved the experimental protocols for animal uses. For spiking of C-dots in serum, blood samples were collected from healthy mice and kept at room temperature for clotting of blood. The coagulate precipitate was removed by centrifuge at 3500rpm for 15min. The upper layer obtained was taken as serum and treated by Acetonitrile for removing high molecular weight proteins [15]. For the quantification of Sr, GA and DA, 25μM of each was added in acetonitrile which was regarded as stock solution. From the stock solution, different concentrations of Sr, GA and DA were added in serum in the range from 1nM to 10nM solution.

In the presence of ultra violet light, pale yellow color of C-dots changed to bright blue colored solution. This change in color is due to availability of energy traps or quantum confinement of electron sequestered within C-dots [22,23]. The absorption of C-dots at the range of 236–290nm in the UV spectra. A sharp peak at 276nm (Fig. 1(a)) was observed because of n →π* transition of electron linked with carbonyl groups [24]. The dual conjugative effects are responsible for this excitation. The first one is in nanometer scale sp2 clusters and the second is from chromophore or colored groups like CC, CC, CO bonds [25–27]. They are excellent matrix because it can absorb laser energy in the range of 330nm. The function of matrix is to absorb laser energy and transfer it to analytes. Thereby, it prevents fragmentation of analytes, resulting in less noises or background interferences. Although this matrix is stable when laser strikes, it does not burn or convert into ashes inside the MALDI-MS.

The fluorescence studies are shown in Fig. 1(b) where the purified C-dots which were used and the excitation based emission spectra of the C-dots solutions were recorded at 453nm when they were excited at wavelengths of 320nm [26,28]. The results displayed unique optical properties of C-dots as reported from previous studies [29]. TEM image (Fig. 1(c)) displayed spherical shaped C-dots ranging from ∼2 to 4nm in the diameter. Smaller size of C-dots among the carbon-based nanomaterial facilitates one to one interaction with the analyte and enhances the maximum transfer of laser energy.

The Raman spectrum of C-dots displayed (Fig. 2(a)) two peaks at around 1350cm−1 and 1540cm−1, which can be attributed to the D-band (sp3) and G-band (sp2). High intensity of D band (distortion band or defect band) is due to the presence of defects in the structure of C-dots. Presence of two bands indicates the amorphous nature of C-dots [30–32]. These defects produced fluorescence in carbon nanoparticles including C-dots. The intensity of the G-band was found to be less prominent; this indicates the presence of less sp2 carbon. Because of these defects, C-dots are like ultra-small oxygenated graphene oxide sheets; these defects formed sp2-sp3-oxygen matrix leading to electron hole pair formation and hence eliminate colors under UV light [33–35]. The presence of Sp2 and Sp3 are distinct features of an ideal carbon based matrix. Due to the structural consistency and conjugated Sp2 bonds in C-dots show sufficient absorption and hence transfer the appropriate amount of energy for an ionize DA, GA and Sr.

The XRD pattern (Fig. 2(b)) of C-dots shows incomplete crystalline or amorphous nature with a broad peak at 2θ =43.12° which is assigned as (101) diffraction pattern of graphitic carbon. The peak at 26.17° shows interlayer spacing of around 3.67Å which is slightly more than that between (002) planes in bulk graphite i.e. 3.44Å [28]. This enhancement in spacing is due to the oxygen functionalized groups on the surface of C-dots. Because of the crystalline nature of C-dots, it can work as an ordered structure for efficient interaction and transfer of laser energy and helps in soft ionization of analytes. These ionized molecules help in the detection of analytes by detector component in the MALDI-MS.

We recorded the FTIR measurements (Fig. 3 ) and a sharp peak at 1350–1400cm−1 appears from the bending of CH bonds of alkane and also CC stretch and NH bends. IR bands at 1740cm−1 (CO vibrational stretch) and broad peak 3200–3500cm−1 (OH vibrational stretch) point out the existence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of C-dots. As per our earlier discussion on origin of optical properties of C-dots, these functional groups must at the edges of C-dots in order to break the energy traps and make electron–hole pairs to contribute in fluorescence.

A noteworthy feature of C-dots with respect to detection of analytes using MALDI-MS is their surface functionalization using COOH and NH2 groups. These groups interact with analytes and may enhance their solubility in water or other solvents in order to transfer the laser energy to the analytes for their sensitive detection.

Due to the excellent structural properties mentioned earlier, C-dots were found to be an efficient matrix for the detection of low molecular weight biomarkers (Sr, GA and DA) in biological samples. In the case of pure C-dots, no background signals can be observed in the spectra, mainly because of the absence of charged moieties in the selected range. This is one of the most exciting properties of C-dots to serve as an ideal matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of C-dots as a matrix by MALDI-MS
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the novelty of C-dots over widely used conventional matrix DHB, we carried out the MALDI-MS of C-dots and pure DHB as shown in Fig. 4A(a) and (b) DHB matrix exhibited chaotic signals whereas C-dots show complete absence of signals. DHB shows the background peaks like m/z 137.124, 178.101, 274.655, 324.107, 339.359, 360.004, 377.272 and 399.359 which also appeared during the analysis of biomarkers. In Fig. 4A(c) the detection of DA (MW=189) was performed by using DHB matrix and the following peaks were observed m/z 137.665, 178.027, 190.532 (M+H)+, 212.107 (M+Na)+, 228.665 (M+K)+, 274.655, 324.127, 339.502, 377.359 along with some of the peaks from DHB matrix which are marked by rhombus sign. In Fig. 4A(d) where C-dots were used as the matrix then only two peaks were obtained m/z 190.325 (M+H)+ and m/z 212.655 (M+Na)+. We observed that using C-dots as the matrix, reduction of the background peaks and fragmentation of peaks using C-dots. The same analyte was also spiked in the serum and detected by C-dots.

Fig. 4B(a) and (b) is obtained from the C-dots and DHB matrix spectra as already explained above the peaks. Fig. 4B(c) exhibits analytes which were already present in the alone matrix. Some unknown (low molecular weight) proteins peaks appeared due to serum. In the spectra of Fig. 4B(d) C-dots matrix shows its capability in removing the unwanted background signals and fragmentation of analytes. The high intensity peak of DA as star mark m/z 190.591 (M+H)+ was observed, whereas five peaks were also observed which originated from serum proteins. It proved that C-dots are a superior matrix than the traditional organic matrix for detection of low molecular weight compounds in the serum also.

When another important analyte for Alzheimer disease was taken Sr, it also shows the fantastic result by detecting it in the very low concentration with less interference of unwanted peaks. In Fig. 5A(a) and (b) for comparison between the two matrices, its spectra were taken for the analysis of the biomarkers and the DHB matrix peaks were assigned by rhombus shaped symbol. In this case, it is observed that the intensity peak of Sr m/z 177.226 (M+H)+ is very low and other fragmentation peaks of the Sr appear along with matrix peaks like m/z 112.937, 137.313, 200.115 (M+Na)+ adduct, 214.949 (M+K)+ adduct, 274.115, 318.199, 339.216, 356.991, 377.298, 399.732. After using the C-dots as a matrix, cumbersome unknown peaks disappeared with less background. The protonation ions of Sr at m/z 177.772 (M+H)+ with high intensity which can be assigned as a biomarker along with 199.38 (M−H+Na)+ adduct and 214.949 (M+K)+ adduct, 232.820. In Fig. 5B(c), many peaks originating from the protonation, fragmentation, or interferences were observed. The peak allotment is the same as discussed earlier. Some of the unknown peaks may be from serum. Here, it is observed that protonation peak of Sr is coinciding with the peak of DHB matrix. The last spectra of this figure give a promising result by screening the unwanted peaks with high intensity of our target protonated peak which is assigned as m/z 177.772 (M+H)+, 215.949 (M+K)+ as a biomarker. Some serum protein peaks were also produced with low abundance like m/z 146.645, 165.652, 192.902 which can affect the result if proper care is not taken such as threshold energy, optimum concentration of analyte and matrix, laser shots and intensity of laser.

There is another very important biomarker for this appalling disease known as GA which is also tried for MALDI-MS detection. We recorded the background of both the matrices which can be seen in Fig. 6A(a) and (b). Again, the C-dots show no background peaks whereas the DHB matrix shows some background peaks which are depicted in the rhombus shape mark. Then we added GA in the DHB matrix for air drying on the MALDI-MS plate for detection. During the analysis, we found that many peaks were generated from these traditional matrixes which are also depicted by the rhombus sign. The GA protonated peak m/z 147.383 (M+H)+ can be easily seen in the spectra of Fig. 6A(c) marked by the star signs. Its abundance is low compared with lots of unwanted fragmentation peaks and matrix peaks. In addition, the sodium and potassium adduct ions are also produced which are assigned as m/z 169.652 (M+Na)+ and m/z 185.1990 (M+K)+ in Fig. 6A(d). Treated with C-dots, the GA assigned for high intensity of sharp peak may be due to the donation of electrons present in the C-dots from the functional group of COOH. This passivation property of C-dots makes them as an excellent matrix for the detection of small molecules in MALDI-MS.

Similar phenomenon was also observed when GA was spiked in the serum. Fig. 6B(a) and (b) are the MALDI-MS spectra of C-dots and DHB matrix which was already explained previously. Many interference peaks appeared by using the DHB matrix in Fig. 6B(c) which are assigned as m/z 126.144, 137.532, 316.670; there are some other unknown peaks which may be coming from serum. Out of these peaks, only few were observed when high efficient C-dots were utilized for detecting the biomarker peaks. It was observed that when this analyte was spiked in the serum, its peak become broad and also several unwanted peaks were produced which may be fragmentation, serum unknown proteins, ionization moieties, etc. It is easy to detect the biomarker peaks from Fig. 6B(d) from this mixture since the peak is still sharp at m/z 147.5 (M+H)+ even the peak from sodium adduct m/z 169.917.

Table 1 shows MALDI-MS spectra of analyte like dopamine hydrochloride, Sr and glutamic acid. It clearly demonstrates the m/z of the peaks whether it is coming from DHB matrix, C-dots or serum. By looking at this, we can easily observe that the C-dots have no background peaks but DHB has several interference peaks; it shows that these interference peaks were produced with analytes resulting in difficulty in identification. But when the C-dots were used as a matrix then selected peaks were produced which allow successful detection of analyte peaks even in the presence of serum.

In order to validate the inherent ability of C-dots to detect and to quantify the amount of DA, Sr and GA in human serum by MALDI-MS; we added various concentrations of dopamine hydrochloride, Sr and GA in separate aliquots. Additionally, excellent mixing of C-dots with biomarkers on target plate gives fine. These reasons motivate to quantify the above biomarkers by using the C-dots as a matrix by using the MALDI-MS. We employed the equation as (LOD=3Sa/b) where Sa is the standard deviation of regression line and b is the slope of calibration curve [32]. Using standard calibration curve for DA (R 2 =0.9838; y =15.012x −0.8111), Sr (R 2 =0.9849; y =14.624x −1.052) and for GA (R 2 =0.9898; y =13.917x −1.438) shown in Supporting information S1.

In order to validate the inherent ability of C-dots to detect and to quantify the amount of DA, Sr and GA in human serum by MALDI-MS; we added various concentrations of dopamine hydrochloride, Sr and GA in separate aliquots. Additionally, excellent mixing of C-dots with biomarkers on target plate gives fine. These reasons motivate to quantify the above biomarkers by using the C-dots as a matrix by using the MALDI-MS. We employed the equation as (LOD=3Sa/b) where Sa is the standard deviation of regression line and b is the slope of calibration curve [32]. Using standard calibration curve for DA (R 2 =0.9838; y =15.012x −0.8111), Sr (R 2 =0.9849; y =14.624x −1.052) and for GA (R 2 =0.9898; y =13.917x −1.438) shown in Supporting information S1.

The limit of detection (LOD) is summarized in Table 2 and relative standard deviation (RSD) of dopamine hydrochloride, Sr and glutamic acid is also presented. This ability of as prepared C-dots explains their practicality in diagnostics for both qualitative and quantitative detection. The LODs of the above biomarkers were reported from literature: Idris et al. found 2.5μgmL−1 for the LOD for DA [36], whereas Pahuja et al. found only 0.35μg/mL. Guell et al. determined the limit of detection of Sr as 500nM [37,38]. These results recommended that C-dots are best matrices in detection and quantification of the above biomarkers, whereas other matrices suffering from non-uniform co-crystallizations with analytes lead to difficulty in reproducibility of peaks. To combat this problem nanoparticles were introduced as a matrix like Au, CdSe, Pt, HgTe, ZnO [39–43], etc. but they are highly toxic in nature and their synthesis is complicated in contrast to C-dots, which are easy to synthesize at low temperature and highly biocompatible. One more important property of a good matrix is a low threshold of laser intensity (LI) [44]. By using C-dots as a matrix the threshold LI is only 12mJ/cm2 for the above three biomarkers. In contrast, by using DHB, this threshold LI reaches to 31mJ/cm2 for the same biomarkers. C-dots contain hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups on its surface, which make them highly soluble in water and help in fast transfer of laser energy to biomarkers. This property of C-dots as a matrix laid the foundation to detect very small molecules in MALDI-MS. In this study, C-dots help in fast detection of biomarkers in very low concentration in serum, which facilitated the quantification of biomarkers.

Highly fluorescent and spherical (water soluble) C-dots were formed by using citric acid and exploited as a MALDI-MS matrix for the detection and quantification of dopamine hydrochloride, serotonin and glutamic acid as potential molecular biomarkers for Alzheimer disease and other neuro-related diseases. C-dots were found to be an excellent source for detection of the above biomarkers with low background interferences. With the help of C-dots, we could perform the quantification of DA, Sr, GA to 3, 5 and 8nM, respectively, with RSD<10%. This study demonstrated that the C-dots can be employed in the near future for the detection of many ultra-specific biomarkers which can result in earliest diagnosis of dreadful diseases like cancer, Alzheimer, congenital disease, etc.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the National Sun Yat-Sen University and Kaohsiung Medical University Joint Research Project (NSYSU-KMU JOINT RESEARCH PROJECT), #NSYSU-KMU (104-I 006) for financial support for this publication.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ijms.2015.10.007.

ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Res. J. Pharm. Biol. Chem. Sci.
